main_summary.summary: Purcell Maurice Conway, born 1948 in St. Augustine, Florida, recounts his entry into civil rights activism as a teenager in the Lincolnville Black community during the 1963-1964 St. Augustine campaign. Conway describes being raised by his great-great-grandmother in Lincolnville, his early work selling papers at the Castillo de San Marcos, and his radicalization through encounters with segregation at the St. Augustine Record office and during a job his mother arranged with a white family. He recounts hearing Florida Memorial College students discuss nonviolent demonstration tactics, his participation in marches under Dr. Robert B. Hayling's leadership, and his witness of beatings at two wade-ins at St. Augustine Beach -- the first ending with him struck in the face by a white teenager and a fellow demonstrator beaten unconscious, the second ending when Florida state troopers ordered by Governor Farris Bryant beat Klan sympathizers out of the water. After the movement, Conway attended a Daytona Beach trade school, married, moved to New York, and served in the NYPD where he was a lead plaintiff in promotional-exam discrimination lawsuits that produced over 300 minority sergeants. He returned to Palm Coast, Florida and continues to advocate for the Galimore Center pool's restoration.
key_themes: Wade-in protests at St. Augustine Beach
main_summary.historical_significance:
Conway is a first-person witness to the St. Augustine wade-ins at the height of the 1964 campaign that produced the iconic images of Klan-versus-state-trooper violence on the beach -- a turning point that helped force passage of the Civil Rights Act. His account specifically documents the second wade-in where Florida state troopers, sent by Governor Farris Bryant, fought hostile whites out of the water before letting demonstrators enter; this is rare first-hand testimony of the federal-and-state pressure that broke local Klan dominance.

Conway's interview also documents the through-line from St. Augustine youth-marcher experience to lifelong civil rights litigation: his NYPD promotional-exam lawsuits produced precedent used by police departments across the United States. The interview supplements the work of Dr. Robert B. Hayling, who joins partway through, and adds a youth-marcher perspective to the better-documented adult-organizer record of the St. Augustine movement.

chapters.title: Growing Up in Lincolnville
chapters.summary: Conway describes his birth in 1948 and his upbringing in Lincolnville, St. Augustine's Black self-sufficient community. He was raised after age five by his great-great-grandmother, of Seminole and Spanish heritage, who taught him respect, honesty, and acknowledgment of elders. He spent his childhood at the little links (the only Black park in St. Augustine, today the Galimore Center) under instructor Mr. Eddie Lee Vickers.

chapters.title: Paper Boy at the Castillo
chapters.summary: At age ten, Conway ventured across Washington Street into the white area to ask the St. Augustine Record for a paper-boy job. After selling poorly in Lincolnville, he discovered the Castillo de San Marcos as a sales location and outsold the white paper boys until two of them followed him and tourists began buying from them instead. He learned the geography of racial commerce in downtown St. Augustine.

chapters.title: High School and Awakening
chapters.summary: Conway describes challenging teachers at R.J. Murray High School over outdated textbooks and reading the medical books left by his uncle Edward Norton. He recounts the high school DCT work-study program and his anger watching white storekeepers and customers address his mother as Julia while she had to say Miss to them. His employer at an Anastasia Island grocery store refused to sign his school paperwork until he threatened to quit.

chapters.title: Florida Memorial Students and Nonviolent Training
chapters.summary: Conway recalls hearing male Florida Memorial College students discuss civil rights demonstration tactics at the segregated college pool, the only one open to Black youth in summers. Drawn initially to Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X, he was persuaded by the college students to embrace nonviolence. At fifteen he committed to demonstrating, telling his mother he had to do this for his future and his children's future.

chapters.title: Lawnmower Job and Family Mobilization
chapters.summary: Conway recounts the day his mother sent him to mow a white friend's yard on Anastasia Island. The white woman served him a sandwich next to her dog's food bowl. His mother, called by Conway from the woman's phone, came to retrieve him and afterward joined the movement. Conway's father and three brothers were all arrested one night while Conway, the only one not jailed, stayed with his great-great-grandmother.

chapters.title: First Wade-in at St. Augustine Beach
chapters.summary: Conway recounts a wade-in at St. Augustine Beach with four other teenagers and a tall white organizer. A white mob converged from north and south. A young Black girl locked herself in the car. Conway was punched in the face by a white teenager and watched the white organizer beaten over the head with a pipe before a lone police car arrived and the mob dispersed. Hosea Williams responded with a larger second wade-in two weeks later.

chapters.title: Second Wade-in and the State Troopers
chapters.summary: Conway describes the second wade-in two weeks later: a cordon of helmeted Florida state troopers, ordered to St. Augustine by Governor Farris Bryant, walked into waist-deep water and beat the Klan and their sympathizers out of the surf with bats and chains. Conway saw the water turn bloody. Dr. Hayling adds that the US Justice Department was also involved in pressuring the state to act.

chapters.title: Movie Theater Confrontation and Police Dog
chapters.summary: Conway describes a young white tutor who took Black children to the downtown movie theater shortly after passage of the Civil Rights Act. When the children fled screaming and a white man on a bicycle chased them with a knife, Conway intervened. Two hulking policemen surrounded him with a German Shepherd; when his Black friends taunted the officers, the police chased them and abandoned Conway, who outran the dog back to Washington Street and hid in a church yard for two days.
related_events: Post-Civil Rights Act testing of integration

chapters.title: Northern College Volunteers
chapters.summary: Conway describes how Northern white college students, including Gail Blattenberg from Princeton, NJ, changed his perspective on whites. The students invited Black St. Augustine youth back to their Northern homes after the tutorial program ended. Conway's mother resisted, but the white students made picket signs in front of the Conway home until she relented. Conway spent two weeks with the Blattenberg family in New Jersey.
related_events: Northern volunteer participation in St. Augustine

chapters.title: Daytona Trade School and Move North
chapters.summary: Conway attended a Daytona Beach junior college trade school after high school and met his wife, who attended Bethune-Cookman. After a year and a half in a brick masonry program, two white students were enrolled and after three months given certificates while Black students were told they had to take an additional six months of carpentry class. Conway's instructor was fired for protesting. Conway refused the settlement, married, and moved to his wife's family home in New York.

chapters.title: NYPD and Promotional-Exam Lawsuits
chapters.summary: After five years in the New York City post office, Conway passed the NYPD exam. He describes the buddy system in NYPD promotions as a racial barrier and his role as a plaintiff in successful lawsuits against the city for discriminatory promotional exams. The sergeants test lawsuit produced over 300 minority sergeants who were called quota sergeants. Conway later filed and won a similar suit on the lieutenants exam; the criteria established in those suits is still used by police departments nationwide.

chapters.title: Return to Florida and Lincolnville Today
chapters.summary: Conway moved back to Palm Coast, Florida. He describes seeing abandoned houses, drug damage, and what he recognizes as red-lining patterns from New York repeating in Lincolnville: banks not lending, taxes rising, gentrification displacing Black residents. His current advocacy focus is reopening the Galimore Center pool, which has been closed over two years; he petitioned the city commissioners with historical context.

chapters.title: Parents and ACCORD Tribute
chapters.summary: Conway pays tribute to his parents Julia and George Conway. He recounts how his father's white butcher coworker at the Anastasia Island grocery store threatened to quit if George was fired for marching, and how a judge greeted his father by name at a local bank during a court-ordered dispute. His mother Julia worked at Flagler Hospital training nurses and was fired before her tenth year to deny her a pension. Conway closes by thanking Mrs. Gwendolyn Duncan and the ACCORD organization, plus his neighbor Mrs. Barbara Vickers who organized the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument.
